title: Chapter 18: The Dinner
content: The elevator opened onto silence.

Not the silence of an empty floor — Maya had worked enough corporate buildings to know that sound. This was deeper, older, the silence of a room that had been designed to absorb everything human. The top floor of Aeterna HQ wasn't corporate. It was a temple with a view.

The corridor stretched ahead of her, marble floors catching the ambient glow of recessed lighting that seemed to generate no heat. The walls were bare — no motivational posters, no art, no branding. Just pale stone and the faint scent of candle wax drifting from somewhere ahead, threaded with something underneath. Copper and soil, like freshly turned earth after rain. Maya's nose wrinkled. She'd smelled that combination once before, in a morgue.

The invitation had arrived at four forty-seven p.m., hand-delivered by Marcus Webb himself. His face had been unreadable. "Mr. Vlad would like you to join him for dinner. A team appreciation event. Seven o'clock, top floor." He'd paused, and something almost human had crossed his features — a flicker in the jaw, a tightening around the eyes. "Wear something professional."
She'd worn what she'd worn to work. Black blazer, dark jeans, boots that could run if they had to. Professional enough.
The dining room announced itself with a single door, no nameplate, no number. Just a handle shaped like a serpent eating its own tail. Maya pushed it open.

Inside: a marble table long enough to seat twelve, set for two. One candle — thick, ivory, unadorned — flickered at the center. The light it threw was insufficient, leaving the room's edges in murk. The walls were the same pale stone as the corridor, broken only by a single painting: a 17th-century Dutch still life, dead game hung from hooks, a hare with glassy eyes, silver tarnished to the color of old bone. The painter had rendered the fur with obsessive precision. It looked soft enough to stroke. It looked like murder.

Vlad was already seated at the near end of the table, his back to the window, the city's evening grid a lattice of gold behind him. He stood when she entered. The motion was too smooth — no trace of the mechanics of standing, no shift of weight, no grunt of effort. One moment seated, the next vertical, like a video edit with a frame missing.
"Ms. Reyes. Thank you for joining me."

His voice hit her before his words did. Too resonant. It filled the room without filling his chest, as though the walls themselves were speaking. She thought of subwoofers. She thought of the 0.7Hz signal Jordi had measured — the frequency below hearing, the one that bypassed the ears and went straight to the viscera. Vlad's voice had overtones she couldn't name.
"Mr. Vlad." She kept her own voice level. She would not be the smaller resonance in this room. "I was told it was a team dinner."
"Indeed." He gestured to the second chair, pulled out slightly, waiting. "The team is you."

The duck was seared to lacquer, the skin crisp and dark, the meat pink at its heart. Roasted vegetables fanned across the plate in colors that belonged in a jewelry store — amethyst beets, emerald beans, carrots the orange of temple bells. The wine was a Burgundy so deep it looked black in the glass, poured from a bottle with no label.
"The vineyard," Vlad said, following her eyes to the bottle. "I've owned it for some time."
Some time. She let that sit. In Vlad-speak, that could mean a decade or a millennium.

She cut into the duck. Brought a piece to her mouth. Chewed. The flavor was extraordinary — fat and salt and smoke, the kind of cooking that cost more per plate than her rent. She barely tasted it. Every nerve in her body was oriented toward the man across from her, the way a compass needle orients toward magnetic north — involuntarily, constantly, with no choice in the matter.

Vlad moved his food. That was the only way to describe it. His fork cut the duck into precise, identical squares. His knife separated the vegetables into smaller and smaller components. He rearranged everything on his plate with the focus of a surgeon, creating patterns — concentric circles, then a grid, then something that looked like a circuit diagram. But he never brought the fork to his mouth. Not once.

Maya watched. She let her gaze rest on his hands — pale, long-fingered, the nails immaculate. They moved with mechanical precision. Lift, cut, rearrange. Lift, cut, rearrange. The food was theatre. He was performing eating the way a deep-cover agent performs normalcy: technically correct, fundamentally hollow.
His eyes, though. His eyes never left her face.

They tracked her as she lifted her own fork, as she chewed, as she reached for the wine. The candlelight caught them and she saw — not reflection. Depth. Pupils that seemed to have no floor, like wells cut into his skull. He watched her mouth when she ate. He watched her throat when she swallowed.
"So," Vlad said, his focus shifting to casual with a speed that made her skin prickle. "Your background. Audio engineering, is that correct? Before the podcast?"
"Among other things."
"And the podcast — 'Unfollowed.' An interesting title. What inspired it?"

She gave him the answer she gave everyone. The deflection about social media, about voice, about reclaiming narrative. He listened with his chin resting on his steepled fingers, and she could tell he'd heard this answer before. From someone. Somewhere. He was testing whether her story was consistent.
"Your interest in audio," he said, when she finished. "Does it extend to infrastructure? Wiring, signal paths, the architecture of sound within buildings?"
The question landed like a scalpel.
"Somewhat," she said. "I know enough to do my job."

"Of course." He picked up his wine glass — he did drink the wine, she noticed; it was only food he avoided — and swirled it, watching the legs run down the bowl. "Aeterna's AV infrastructure is extensive. State of the art. I insisted on it personally. Sound is the most intimate sense, wouldn't you agree? It enters through the ear without permission. It reshapes the brain. Infrasound, ultrasound — frequencies we can't consciously perceive, yet they alter us at the cellular level." He took a measured sip. "Fascinating territory for someone with your particular interests."
He let the silence breathe for a full ten seconds. Then:
"I understand you've been spending time in the AV corridors. Third floor, east wing. Near the restricted elevator bank."

A wire in Maya's chest went taut. She felt it physically — a stretching, a pulling, the sensation of a tripwire under tension. He knew about the transmitters. Or he was fishing. The difference might not matter.
She kept her face neutral. It took everything she had. "The AV infrastructure runs through the whole building. I go where the cables go."
Vlad smiled.

It was a beautiful smile, proportioned and symmetrical, and it ended at the boundary of his mouth. His eyes remained flat, reptilian, the eyes of something watching from inside a human suit. "Of course. Cables. The nervous system of any modern building." He tilted his wine glass, watching the light refract through burgundy. "Fascinating how much information flows through them, isn't it? Every cable a pathway. Every junction a node. And how vulnerable that makes the system. One severed connection, and the whole network begins to atrophy."
He was telling her he knew. Not everything — he wouldn't show his hand that completely. But enough. Enough to let her know the walls had ears.
Enough to let her know she was inside something that could close around her.
"Fortunately," Maya said, "I'm just an audio engineer."
Vlad's smile widened by a millimeter. "Yes. Just an audio engineer."

The duck sat cooling on her plate. She hadn't touched it in minutes. The wine in her glass was untouched — she'd been lifting it to her lips but not drinking, a mirror of his performance with the food, and she wondered if he'd noticed the symmetry. Probably. He noticed everything.
Vlad set down his glass. The click against marble was precise, surgical.
"Your friend Lucy," he said. "Remarkable woman."
The temperature in the room dropped. It wasn't the air — it was Maya's blood, retreating from her extremities, pooling somewhere deep and primitive where fight-or-flight lived.

"Such vitality," Vlad continued. He spoke the way a sommelier speaks of a vintage — with appreciation, with authority, with the certainty of ownership. "Such... resilience. She's been through a great deal. But she adapts." A pause, weighted. "They all adapt eventually."

Maya's hand closed around the stem of her wine glass. The crystal was cold, colder than it should have been from ambient temperature, as though the glass itself was pulling heat from her skin. She wanted to throw it. She wanted the burgundy to splash across his white shirt, she wanted the crystal to shatter against that composed face, she wanted to watch something break through that mask.
"What happened to Lucy, Mr. Vlad?"
He tilted his head. The gesture was avian. A bird studying something it was about to eat.
"Miss Chen is receiving the finest care available. She is, in fact, thriving." The word crawled across Maya's spine. "You could say she's found her purpose."

The horror of it wasn't in the threat. It was in the sincerity. He meant it. He believed it with the conviction of centuries — that what he'd done to Lucy Chen was a gift, a transformation, an elevation from the meaningless churn of ordinary life into something purer. He had consumed her and called it care. He had hollowed her out and called it purpose.

The copper-soil smell was stronger now. Maya realized with a sick lurch that it was coming from him. Not his cologne — his skin. The very old don't smell like the living. They smell like the things the living will become.

Vlad stood. The chair didn't scrape — he'd risen without disturbing it, without friction, as though the laws of physics had made an exception. He walked to the window, and the city unfolded below them like a circuit board, thousands of lights marking thousands of lives, all of them unaware of the thing standing forty stories above their heads.

"Ms. Reyes." He faced the glass. His reflection looked back at her — gaunt, composed, inhumanly still. "I have lived a very long time. I have seen empires rise and fall. I have watched humanity repeat the same mistakes with tedious regularity." He turned from the window. "Do you know what I have learned?"
The light shifted. The candle guttered in some imperceptible draft.
"The powerful take. The weak are taken. That is not cruelty. It is nature. The lion does not apologize to the gazelle."
Maya looked at his shadow.
It was still by the window.

He'd turned. He'd moved three feet from the glass, was standing in the center of the room now, his body angled toward her. But his shadow remained against the window — the silhouette of a man gazing out at the city, arms at his sides, head slightly tilted. It didn't move. It didn't shrink or stretch with the change in distance from the light source. It was fixed, like a photograph, like a stain.

Her stomach dropped through the floor. Every rational explanation she tried to construct collapsed on contact with the reality of what she was seeing. Light doesn't work that way. Shadows don't work that way. But this one did, because it wasn't a shadow. It was something else. A residue. An echo. The shape a man leaves when he's stood in one place for a very long time.
She dragged her eyes back to his face. She would not look at the shadow again. If she looked at it, she would scream.
"You're not a lion, Mr. Vlad." Her voice didn't waver. Something in her chest was crystallizing — not ice, something harder, something geological. "You're a parasite."

The change in him was subtle. A flicker — not anger, not offense. Interest. His pupils dilated by a fraction. The planes of his face seemed to sharpen, as though the word had stripped away a layer of the performance and revealed something underneath. Something that liked being seen.
He smiled, and this time it was a different smile — slower, realer, more terrible.
"And yet," he said softly, "the parasite survives long after the host is forgotten."

He returned to his chair. He sat. Somewhere in that motion, the impossible shadow by the window was simply gone — she didn't see it leave, didn't see it move, looked up and the window was just a window, the city just a city, as though her brain had agreed to edit the footage and never speak of it again.
"You have spirit, Ms. Reyes." He folded his hands on the table. "I find that... appealing."

The word dropped into the silence like a stone into deep water. It wasn't flirtation — there was nothing sexual in it, nothing that belonged to the vocabulary of human desire. It was older. Hunger that had outlasted the species it first fed on. The hunger of something that eats because eating is what it does, because the alternative is cessation, and cessation is the only thing it fears.
"I am going to make you an offer," Vlad said. "One final time."
The finality was a wall. She felt it close behind her.

"End your investigation. Delete your podcast. Accept the position I have offered." He paused, and the pause was surgical — he was giving her time to feel the weight of each condition before the next landed. "And I will ensure that Lucy Chen is released. Unharmed. You have my word."

Maya's hands had gone still. The wine glass sat in her peripheral vision, forgotten. The marble table was cold under her forearms — the cold of stone that had never known warmth, that had been cut from the earth and polished into a surface for transactions like this one.
"Your word," she said.
"I have kept every promise I've ever made." His voice dropped, and the resonance filled her skull, pressed against the inside of her temples. "For five hundred years."

Lucy. Lucy in exchange for silence. Lucy in exchange for everything she'd built, everything she'd uncovered, every woman in Lumina's catalog, every 0.7Hz transmission, every drop of blood on the ledger. Take the deal and save one life. Refuse the deal and keep fighting for all of them — but Lucy stays in the dark.
She thought of Lucy's voice on that recording. The flatness. The emptiness where a person used to be.
Maya stood. The chair scraped this time — she made it scrape, she pushed back hard enough to claim the sound, to fill the room with something human and imperfect and loud.
"And how many of those promises," she said, "were made to people who are still alive to hear them kept?"

Vlad's expression didn't change. But something behind his eyes shifted, a recalibration, a predator reclassifying its prey. She watched him reassess her — not as a problem to manage but as something to want. Something worth taking time over.
She turned and walked.

Not fast. Not slow. She walked the way you walk out of a room you never want to enter again, when running would mean admitting you'd been trapped. Her boots struck marble — one, two, three, steady, deliberate. Through the doorway. Into the corridor. The candlelight receded behind her and the recessed lighting took over, antiseptic and flat.

The laugh came from the dining room. Low, resonant, without mirth. It rolled down the corridor after her like a slow wave, like a heartbeat amplified beyond the body, beyond the room, beyond the building. It didn't chase her — it waited for her, the way sound waits, patient and inevitable, knowing she'd hear it long after she'd stopped listening.

She reached the elevator. Pressed the button. Her hand was shaking — she could see it, a fine tremor that turned her fingers to blur at the edges. She pressed the shaking hand against her thigh to still it. The doors opened. She stepped inside. Turned. Faced the closing doors. The corridor stretched away, empty and pale and smelling of copper and soil.
The doors closed.

Maya leaned against the elevator wall. The metal was cold through her blazer. She pressed her palms flat against it — left, right, grounding herself in the physical, in the real, in the things that still obeyed the laws of thermodynamics and optics and the basic fucking principle that shadows move when people move.
Her phone buzzed. She pulled it out with hands that didn't want to cooperate.
Jordi: *Paper's on hold. Legal sent a cease-and-desist at 6pm. We need Kapoor. This week.*
Seventeen days until the IPO.
Seventeen days until Aeterna's blood machine went public, its catalog normalized, its women — Lucy's women — reduced to line items on a balance sheet.
The elevator descended. Maya watched the floor numbers drop. Her hands were still shaking.

She put the phone away. She straightened her blazer. She breathed in through her nose and out through her mouth the way her therapist had taught her, a lifetime ago, in a life before Aeterna, before 0.7Hz, before shadows that didn't move and men who didn't eat and offers that sounded like salvation and tasted like a trap.
Lucy. In exchange for silence.

The elevator reached the ground floor. The doors opened. Maya walked out into the lobby, through the glass doors, into the October night. The air smelled like traffic and eucalyptus and nothing like copper and soil.
Seventeen days.
